rulings.Case Title: R & G EXECUTIVE LOUNGE, INC. VS. HENRY HUNG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, May 9, 2019, Line 3, CROSS DEFENDANT R & G EXECUTIVE LOUNGE, INC.'s DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT. Cross-Defendant R&G Executive Lounge, Inc.'s demurrer to Cross-Complainant's cross-complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to the sole cause of action for unreimbursed business expenses under Labor Code section 2802. Labor Code section 2802 does not require an employer to indemnify its employees for expenditures or losses arising out of a lawsuit brought by the employer. (Thornton v. Cal. Unemployment Ins. Appeals Bd. (2012) 204 Cal.App.4th 1403, 1420 ["[U]nder Labor Code section 2802 . . . an employee sued by his employer is not entitled to reimbursement for the costs of successfully defending against the lawsuit because the reimbursement obligation is phrased in terms of indemnity . . . which implies an obligation to pay a judgment entered or expenses incurred in a lawsuit by a third party."]; Nicholas Laboratories LLC v. Chen (2011) 199 Cal.App.4th 1240, 1251 ["We are not persuaded that the Legislature, in drafting section 2802, intended to depart from the usual meaning of the word 'indemnify' to address 'first party' disputes between employers and employees. . . . The fact that the Legislature did not do so suggests disputes between employers and employees are subject to the ordinary rules applying to the recovery of attorney fees in California litigation."].) Although Joe Ling and Henry Hung's cross-complaint originally pled individual claims in addition to claims pled derivatively on behalf of R&G, Defendants have since voluntarily dismissed the individual claims. (RJN Ex. F.) The sixth and seventh causes of action for fraud and conspiracy to commit breach of fiduciary duty remain alleged on a derivative basis and no other substantive changes have been made to the allegations of Ling and Hung's cross-complaint. (RJN Exs. A, F.) Thus, any expenditures by Cross-Complainant in defense of these claims relate equally to the derivative claims. As a result, Cross-Complainant's claim for unreimbursed business expenses is improperly sought for defending against claims brought by the employer, R&G, rather than claims brought by a third party. The cases relied upon by Cross-Complainant are therefore inapplicable because those cases involve seeking indemnity for defending against third-party lawsuits. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for R&G is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GILBERT PAPAZIAN II VS. KEVIN MICHAEL SULLIVAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Kevin Sullivan To Serve Original Verified Responses To Special Interrogatories (Set 2) Without Objections For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 9, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF GILBERT PAPAZIAN's Motion To Compel Kevin Sullivan To Serve Original Verified Responses To Special Interrogatories (Set 2) Without Objections For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Adrienne Rogers, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties ordered to appear. Parties should be prepared to discuss whether defendant Kevin Sullivan has provided a verified response to plaintiff's Special Interrogatories (Set 2), and whether monetary sanctions against Sullivan are appropriate. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 2030.290; Sinaiko Healthcare Consulting, Inc. v. Pacific Healthcare Consultants (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 390, 408-09.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to adrienneleight@hotmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CHEW KAM KUNG VS. JINCONG ZHEN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Jincong Zhen, Sam Wing Construction, Inc.'S Demurrer To 3rd Amended Complaint.(Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, May 9, 2019, Line 5, Defendant Jincong Zhen, Sam Wing Construction, Inc.'S Demurrer To 3rd Amended Complaint.(Part 2 Of 2 For Tentative Ruling Only). The cause of action for a petition to remove mechanics lien pursuant to Civil Code § 8480(a) fails to state a cause of action because defendants did commence an action for foreclosure on the mechanics lien and the Alameda Superior Court issued a judgment in their favor on Nov. 12, 2013. (RJN, Exh. D). The cause of action is barred by res judicata. Because Mr. Kung has not shown how an amendment could cure the deficiencies in the first, fourth and fifth causes of action, the demurrer as to these causes of action is sustained without leave to amend. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CHEW KAM KUNG VS. JINCONG ZHEN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, May 9, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT JINCONG ZHEN, SAM WING CONSTRUCTION, INC.'s DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT.(part 1 of 2) Defendants Jincong Zhen, Sam Wing Construction, Inc.'s demurrer to the third amended complaint by plaintiff Chew Kam Kung is overruled as to the third cause of action for construction defects against Mr. Zhen only; and sustained without leave to amend as to the first, second, fourth, and fifth causes of action for breach of contract, unlicensed contractor, slander of title, and petition to remove mechanics lien. As the court previously held in ruling on the demurrer to the first amended complaint, Mr. Kung has pled a viable claim for negligence (construction defects) against Mr. Zhen which needs to be restated verbatim in the operative pleading and not incorporated by reference. The new allegation against Defendant Sam Wing Construction, Inc. that it installed 20-year-life roof shingles rather than 30-year-life shingles as "agreed on" (TAC 29) is a disguised claim for breach of an oral contract that is barred by the statute of limitations; accordingly, the demurrer of Sam Wing Construction to that cause of action is sustained without leave to amend. The breach of contract cause of action against both defendants based on an written 2010 contract and an oral 2012 contract is barred by the applicable four- and two-year statutes of limitations respectively (Code Civ. Proc. §§ 337(a), 339), because the alleged breach, the failure to finalize work, occurred by March 2010 (TAC 13, 22). The cause of action for "unlicensed contractor" is time-barred on its face. Any claim Mr. Kung might have under Bus. & Prof. Code § 7031 to recover compensation paid to unlicensed contractors is barred by the four-year statute of limitations. (Code Civ. Proc. § 343; TAC 13 [alleging last check was given to Sam Wing Construction, Inc. on February 27, 2012].) The facts alleged do not support a viable slander of title claim. The statute of limitations has run on the mechanics lien recorded in 2012. (Code Civ. Proc. § 338(g).) Further, there is a court judgment ordering foreclosure on the lien (RJN, Exh. D), and the levy by the Alameda County Sheriff, although recorded in 2019, cannot support a slander of title because it is a levy in execution of a sale for real property pursuant to a judgment. (See Deutsche Bank National Trust Co. v. Pyle (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 513, 528 n.7 ["The elements of a cause of action for slander of title are (1) a publication, which is (2) without privilege or justification and thus with express or implied malice, (3) false, either knowingly so or made without regard to its truthfulness and (4) causes pecuniary loss."].) (end or part 1 of 2)=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ECOLOGICAL RIGHTS FOUNDATION VS. ARETT SALES CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And For Entry Of Consent Judgment As To Defendant Nantucket Pavers, Inc.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, May 9, 2019, Line 6, PLAINTIFF ECOLOGICAL RIGHTS FOUNDATION's Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And For Entry Of Consent Judgment As To Defendant Nantucket Pavers, Inc. Plaintiff Ecological Rights Foundation's [corrected] motion to approve Proposition 65 settlement and for entry of consent order as to Nantucket Pavers, Inc. is granted. No opposition filed and the settlement complies with the requirements for Proposition 65 settlements under Health & Safety Code section 25249.7. The Court finds that the warning that is required by the settlement complies with Health & Safety Code sections 25249.5-25249.14; the award of attorney's fees is reasonable under California law; and the penalty amount is reasonable based on the criteria set forth in Health & Safety Code section 25249.7(b)(2). The Court will sign the corrected proposed order submitted by the moving party. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: RACHEL ARCHULETA VS. ARYAN KENCHIN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Enforce Stipulated Settlement Agreement Pursuant To C.C.P. 664.6, For An Order Compelling Performance And For Sanctions In The Amount Of $1,996.00, As Against Defendant Aryan Kenchin And His Attorneys Of Record Sheena Patel And John Ranucci Per C.C.P. 128.5; Memorandum Of Points & Authorities; Decl. Of George M. Morris, Esq., In Support Thereof; [Proposed] Order
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, May 9, 2019, Line 12, PLAINTIFF RACHEL ARCHULETA's Motion To Enforce Stipulated Settlement Agreement Pursuant To C.C.P. 664.6, For An Order Compelling Performance And For Sanctions In The Amount Of $1,996.00, As Against Defendant Aryan Kenchin And His Attorneys Of Record Sheena Patel And John Ranucci Per C.C.P. 128.5. Plaintiff Rachel Archuleta's motion to enforce settlement is denied. For Code of Civil Procedure § 664.6 to apply, the parties' agreement for settlement of the case must be "in a writing signed by the parties outside the presence of the court or orally before the court." (Code Civ. Proc. § 664.6.) The term "parties" means the litigants themselves, not their attorneys of record. (Levy v. Superior Court (1995) 10 Cal.4th 578, 586.) However, when the defense is being provided without reservation of rights by an insurer, and it has the contractual right to settle without the insured's consent, the signature or oral stipulation by insurance defense counsel is sufficient. (Fiege v. Cooke (2004) 125 Cal.App.4th 1350, 1353-1355; Robertson v. Chen (1996) 44 Cal.App.4th 1290, 1293-96.) In this case, neither Defendant Aryan Kenchin nor the insurer signed the release agreement, and the record indicates that the insurer was reserving its rights, so the exception does not apply. (Ranucci Decl., 3-4.) The court denies both parties' requests for sanctions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ECOLOGICAL RIGHTS FOUNDATION VS. YODER SMOKERS, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And For Entry Of Consent Judgment As To Defendant Yoder Smokers, Inc.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, May 9, 2019, Line 13, PLAINTIFF ECOLOGICAL RIGHTS FOUNDATION's Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And For Entry Of Consent Judgment As To Defendant Yoder Smokers, Inc. Plaintiff Ecological Rights Foundation's motion to approve Proposition 65 settlement and for entry of consent judgment as to Yoder Smokers, Inc. is granted. No opposition filed and the settlement complies with the requirements for Proposition 65 settlements under Health & Safety Code section 25249.7. The Court finds that the warning that is required by the settlement complies with Health & Safety Code sections 25249.5-25249.14; the award of attorney's fees is reasonable under California law; and the penalty amount is reasonable based on the criteria set forth in Health & Safety Code section 25249.7(b)(2). The Court will sign the proposed order submitted by the moving party. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SAN DONG BEST INC. ET AL VS. GENERAL TSO KITCHEN, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Establishing Admissions Or Compelling Responses And Further Responses, And Compelling Responses And Further Responses To Their First Set Of Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories, And Requests For Production Of Documents, And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 9, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANTs GENERAL TSO KITCHEN, INC., and LI CAO's Motion For An Order Establishing Admissions Or Compelling Responses And Further Responses, And Compelling Responses And Further Responses To Their First Set Of Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories, And Requests For Production Of Documents, And For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Adrienne Rogers, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The court finds San Dong Best Inc. and Shubo Wang's (collectively, plaintiffs) responses to General Tso Kitchen, Inc. and Li Cao's (collectively, defendants) discovery requests were timely served. Parties ordered to appear. Parties should be prepared to meet and confer on (1) the date by which plaintiffs will provide supplemental responses to form interrogatories 3.6 and 3.7 to Best, and form interrogatories 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.5, 11.1, and 11.2 to Wang; (2) the date by which plaintiffs will produce responsive documents "that are not protected from disclosure by law" and the appropriate verifications to the extent the verifications have not already been provided (Code Civ. Proc., § 2031.250(a)); and (3) whether plaintiffs will provide supplemental responses to special interrogatories. The parties should be prepared to discuss their competing requests for sanctions. Defendants' request for order deeming requests for admissions admitted, or to compel further responses to requests for admission is DENIED. Plaintiffs' responses were timely served and Code compliant. (Code Civ. Proc., § 2033.210; St. Mary v. Superior Court (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 779.) Defendants' request to compel further response to requests for production Nos. 6 and 18 is DENIED. (See Hofmann Corp. v. Superior Court (1985) 172 Cal.App.3d 357, 362.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to adrienneleight@hotmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
